storage_instructions: Store in a paper bag in the refrigerator for up to 3 weeks. Leave husks on until ready to use.

companions.name: Marigold
companions.notes: Repels nematodes, aphids, and many garden pests

antagonists.name: Fennel
antagonists.notes: Fennel releases compounds that inhibit most plant growth

growing_tips: Plant at least two plants for cross-pollination, as tomatillos are not reliably self-fertile
Stake or cage plants to keep fruit off the ground
Tomatillos are prolific self-seeders; harvest before fruits drop to control volunteers

description: Small, sweet, golden berries enclosed in a papery husk with a tropical, pineapple-like flavor. A unique addition to the home garden and excellent for preserves.
harvest_instructions: Harvest when the husk turns tan and the fruit drops from the plant. Ripe fruits are golden-yellow and sweet.
storage_instructions: Store in husks at room temperature for 1-2 weeks or refrigerate for up to a month. Remove husks before eating.

antagonists.name: Fennel
antagonists.notes: Fennel releases compounds that inhibit most plant growth

growing_tips: Start seeds early indoors as ground cherries need a long growing season
They self-sow readily; mulch around plants to make fallen fruit easier to find
Support sprawling plants with small cages or let them spread as a ground cover

description: Fast-growing Chinese cabbage with thick, juicy white stems and dark green leaves. Versatile in stir-fries, soups, and salads.
harvest_instructions: Cut the whole plant at the base when heads are firm, or harvest individual outer leaves for a cut-and-come-again approach.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a plastic bag for up to 1 week. Wrap loosely to prevent moisture buildup.
growing_tips: Best grown as a spring or fall crop to avoid bolting in summer heat
Use shade cloth if temperatures exceed 75°F
Baby bok choy varieties mature faster and are ideal for small gardens

description: Leafy green with a slightly bitter flavor, available in curly (frisee) and broad-leaf (escarole) types. Adds texture and complexity to salads.
harvest_instructions: Cut the entire head at the soil line when fully formed. For less bitterness, blanch by tying outer leaves over the center 2-3 weeks before harvest.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a plastic bag for up to 1 week. Wash just before use.
growing_tips: Blanching the heads by covering reduces bitterness and produces pale, tender hearts
Grows best in cool weather; plant in early spring or late summer for fall harvest
Consistent moisture prevents bolting and excessive bitterness

description: Italian chicory with striking ruby-red leaves and white ribs, forming tight heads. Its bold, pleasantly bitter flavor is prized in salads and grilled dishes.
harvest_instructions: Cut the head at the base when it feels firm and has colored up. Heads often improve after a light frost.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a plastic bag for up to 2 weeks. Keeps longer than most salad greens.
growing_tips: Fall-planted radicchio produces the best heads with the deepest color
Light frosts sweeten the flavor and intensify the red color
Some varieties can be cut and will regrow a smaller second head

description: Peppery, fast-growing leafy green available in green, red, and purple varieties. Young leaves are mild for salads; mature leaves develop bold, spicy heat.
harvest_instructions: Pick individual outer leaves when 4-6 inches long for baby greens, or harvest the whole plant when mature. Cut-and-come-again method works well.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate unwashed in a plastic bag for up to 5 days. Blanch and freeze for longer storage.
growing_tips: One of the fastest brassicas to mature, ideal for succession planting
Bolts quickly in warm weather; grow in spring and fall
Extremely cold-hardy and can be grown under row cover well into winter

description: Low-growing Asian green forming attractive, flat rosettes of dark green, spoon-shaped leaves. Mild mustard flavor excellent raw or cooked.
harvest_instructions: Harvest outer leaves individually or cut the entire rosette at the base. Plants will regrow from the center if cut above the growing point.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a plastic bag for up to 5 days. Best used fresh.
growing_tips: Very cold-hardy; can survive temperatures down to 15°F with protection
Ideal for spring, fall, and even winter production under cover
Compact rosette habit makes it excellent for containers and small spaces

description: Delicate Japanese mustard green with deeply serrated, feathery leaves and a mild, peppery flavor. A staple in Asian cuisine and mesclun mixes.
harvest_instructions: Cut individual leaves or cut the whole plant 1 inch above the crown for regrowth. Baby leaves are ready in about 20 days.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a damp paper towel inside a plastic bag for up to 5 days.
growing_tips: More bolt-resistant than many other Asian greens, tolerating some heat
Excellent for cut-and-come-again harvesting over several weeks
Grows well in containers and as an interplanting between slower crops

description: Aquatic or semi-aquatic green with a crisp texture and peppery, slightly tangy flavor. Highly nutritious and traditionally grown in running water.
harvest_instructions: Snip stems above the water line, taking no more than one-third of the plant at a time. Harvest before flowering for best flavor.
storage_instructions: Stand stems in a glass of water in the refrigerator, loosely covered. Use within 3-4 days.
growing_tips: Can be grown in a container kept in a shallow tray of water that is changed daily
Prefers cool, running water but adapts to consistently moist garden soil
Thrives in partial shade and cool temperatures; bolts in heat

description: Delicate, cold-hardy salad green also known as corn salad or lamb's lettuce. Forms small rosettes of soft, rounded leaves with a mild, nutty flavor.
harvest_instructions: Cut the entire rosette at the base when leaves are 2-3 inches long. Can also harvest individual leaves.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a damp paper towel inside a sealed bag for up to 5 days. Very delicate; handle gently.
growing_tips: Thrives in cold weather and can overwinter with minimal protection in zones 6+
Sow in late summer or early fall for winter and early spring harvests
Slow to germinate in warm soil; refrigerate seeds before summer sowing

description: Large, sweet root vegetable resembling a turnip but with yellow-orange flesh and a richer, sweeter flavor. Excellent roasted, mashed, or added to soups and stews.
harvest_instructions: Harvest when roots are 3-5 inches in diameter. Flavor improves after light frosts. Pull or dig roots carefully to avoid damage.
storage_instructions: Remove tops and store in a cool, humid root cellar for up to 4-6 months. Refrigerate in a perforated bag for several weeks.
growing_tips: Best sown in midsummer for fall harvest, as flavor sweetens with cool weather and frost
Requires a longer season than turnips; plan accordingly
Thin seedlings early to prevent crowding and encourage large root development

description: Large, mild-flavored Asian radish that can grow over a foot long. Used fresh, pickled, and cooked, and also serves as an excellent cover crop for breaking compacted soil.
harvest_instructions: Pull roots when 8-14 inches long and 2-3 inches in diameter. Best harvested before hard frost. Greens are also edible.
storage_instructions: Remove tops and refrigerate in a plastic bag for up to 2 weeks. Can be stored in damp sand in a root cellar for months.
growing_tips: Best planted in late summer for fall harvest to avoid bolting
Deep, loose soil is essential for long, straight roots
Also used as a 'tillage radish' cover crop to break up compacted subsoil

description: Celery relative grown for its knobby, dense root rather than its stalks. Rich, celery-like flavor makes it ideal for soups, purees, and remoulade.
harvest_instructions: Harvest after first light frosts when roots are 3-4 inches in diameter. Cut off tops and side roots. Trim the rough outer skin before cooking.
storage_instructions: Store in a root cellar or refrigerator in a perforated bag for 3-4 months. One of the best-storing root vegetables.
growing_tips: Requires a very long season; start seeds indoors 10-12 weeks before last frost
Seeds need light to germinate; press onto soil surface and keep moist
Remove lower side shoots throughout the season to encourage a single, large root

description: Vigorous perennial grown for its pungent, sinus-clearing white root. One of the most potent flavors in the garden, used as a condiment with meats and in sauces.
harvest_instructions: Dig roots in late fall after a hard frost for the most pungent flavor. Use a garden fork to loosen soil deeply around the plant.
storage_instructions: Store unwashed roots in damp sand in a cool root cellar for several months. Refrigerate wrapped in damp towels for a few weeks. Prepared horseradish keeps for months in the refrigerator.

companions.name: Potato
companions.notes: Repels potato beetles

growing_tips: Grown from root cuttings, not seed; plant pieces of root at a 45-degree angle
Extremely vigorous and can become invasive; consider planting in a buried container
Harvest all roots annually to control spread, or dedicate a permanent bed

description: Old-fashioned root vegetable known as the 'oyster plant' for its mild, oyster-like flavor when cooked. Produces long, slender, cream-colored roots.
harvest_instructions: Dig roots in late fall or early spring after overwintering. Roots are about 1 inch in diameter and up to 12 inches long. Use a fork to avoid breaking.
storage_instructions: Store in damp sand in a root cellar for several months. Roots can also be left in the ground and dug as needed through winter with mulch cover.
growing_tips: Requires deep, loose, stone-free soil for straight, uniform roots
Use fresh seed each year as viability drops quickly
Flavor improves after frost; many gardeners overwinter roots in the ground for spring harvest

description: Tropical legume grown for its crisp, sweet, starchy tuber. Refreshing eaten raw with lime and chili, or cooked in stir-fries. Requires a long, hot growing season.
harvest_instructions: Harvest before the first frost by digging up tubers. Only the tuber is edible; seeds, leaves, and stems are toxic.
storage_instructions: Store at cool room temperature in a dry place for up to 2 months. Refrigerate cut jicama in water for up to 1 week.
growing_tips: Needs at least 5 months of warm weather; best suited for zones 8+ or started early indoors
Pinch off flowers to redirect energy into tuber growth
All parts except the tuber are toxic; handle with care and do not eat seeds or leaves

description: Tropical plant grown for its aromatic, spicy rhizome used extensively in cooking and herbal medicine. Can be grown in containers and brought indoors in cooler climates.
harvest_instructions: Harvest baby ginger after 4-6 months or mature ginger after 8-10 months when leaves begin to yellow. Carefully dig around the plant to lift rhizomes.
storage_instructions: Store unpeeled ginger in the refrigerator for up to 3 weeks. Freeze for long-term storage. Can also be dried and ground.
growing_tips: Start with organic grocery store ginger or seed ginger from a garden supplier
Thrives in rich, well-drained soil with consistent moisture and humidity
Excellent container plant in cold climates; bring indoors before frost

description: Tropical plant with striking foliage, grown for its bright orange rhizome prized for its color, earthy flavor, and anti-inflammatory properties. Closely related to ginger.
harvest_instructions: Harvest after 8-10 months when foliage dies back. Carefully dig up the clump and break off rhizomes. Wear gloves as turmeric stains skin and clothing.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate fresh rhizomes in a sealed bag for up to 2 weeks. Dry and grind for long-term storage. Can also be frozen whole.
growing_tips: Start rhizome pieces indoors in early spring in zones below 9
Needs warm temperatures consistently above 65°F to grow well
Excellent container plant for northern gardeners; bring indoors in fall

description: Cool-season legume producing large, meaty beans inside thick, velvety pods. A staple in Mediterranean cuisine and an excellent cover crop that fixes nitrogen.
harvest_instructions: Pick pods when beans inside are plump but before the pod turns black. Shell beans and remove the outer skin of each bean for the sweetest flavor.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate shelled beans for up to 5 days. Blanch and freeze for long-term storage. Dried beans store for over a year.

companions.name: Potato
companions.notes: Beans fix nitrogen; potatoes repel Mexican bean beetle

antagonists.name: Onion
antagonists.notes: Alliums inhibit bean growth

antagonists.name: Garlic
antagonists.notes: Garlic stunts bean growth

growing_tips: One of the few legumes that prefers cool weather; plant very early spring or fall
Pinch off growing tips when plants begin to flower to reduce aphid problems and direct energy to pods
Inoculate seeds with rhizobia bacteria for best nitrogen fixation

description: Warm-season legume producing flat, kidney-shaped beans prized for their creamy, buttery texture. Available in bush and pole types.
harvest_instructions: For fresh limas, pick when pods are plump and bright green. For dried limas, leave pods on the plant until they turn brown and rattle.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate fresh shelled beans for up to 1 week. Blanch and freeze for longer storage. Dried beans keep for over a year in an airtight container.

companions.name: Cucumber
companions.notes: Beans fix nitrogen for heavy-feeding cucumbers

companions.name: Carrot
companions.notes: Beans fix nitrogen that carrots utilize

companions.name: Celery
companions.notes: Beans provide nitrogen; shade protects celery

antagonists.name: Onion
antagonists.notes: Alliums inhibit bean growth

antagonists.name: Garlic
antagonists.notes: Garlic stunts bean growth

antagonists.name: Fennel
antagonists.notes: Fennel releases compounds that inhibit most plant growth

growing_tips: Requires warm soil above 65°F for good germination; do not plant too early
Pole varieties need sturdy support and produce over a longer season
Avoid overhead watering during flowering to improve pod set

description: Immature soybeans harvested in the green stage, with a sweet, nutty flavor. A protein-rich snack popular in East Asian cuisine, now widely grown in home gardens.
harvest_instructions: Pick when pods are plump and bright green, before they begin to yellow. Harvest the entire plant at once by pulling it up or cutting at the base.
storage_instructions: Blanch pods in boiling water for 5 minutes, then freeze. Fresh pods keep in the refrigerator for a few days.

companions.name: Potato
companions.notes: Beans fix nitrogen; potatoes repel Mexican bean beetle

companions.name: Cucumber
companions.notes: Beans fix nitrogen for heavy-feeding cucumbers

antagonists.name: Onion
antagonists.notes: Alliums inhibit bean growth

antagonists.name: Garlic
antagonists.notes: Garlic stunts bean growth

growing_tips: Choose short-season varieties for zones 5 and below
Inoculate with soybean-specific rhizobia for best nitrogen fixation
Does not transplant well; direct sow for best results

description: Cool-season legume producing small, lens-shaped seeds in short pods. One of the oldest cultivated crops, valued for its high protein content and fast cooking time.
harvest_instructions: Harvest when the lower pods are brown and dry but before they shatter. Pull or cut entire plants and hang upside down to finish drying.
storage_instructions: Store dried lentils in airtight containers in a cool, dry place for up to a year or more.
growing_tips: Plant early in spring as lentils prefer cool growing conditions
Plants are short and wispy; interplant with a grain like oats for support
Drought-tolerant once established; reduce watering after flowering

description: Warm-season legume producing round, nutty-flavored beans used in hummus, falafel, curries, and salads. Also known as garbanzo bean.
harvest_instructions: For dried chickpeas, let pods dry on the plant until they rattle. For fresh green chickpeas, pick when pods are plump and green.
storage_instructions: Store dried chickpeas in airtight containers for up to a year. Fresh green chickpeas should be eaten within a few days.
growing_tips: Prefers drier conditions than most garden crops; avoid overhead watering
Sensitive to high humidity which can promote blight; provide good air circulation
Each pod contains only 1-2 beans, so plant generously for a worthwhile harvest

description: Heat-loving legume encompassing black-eyed peas, crowder peas, and cream peas. Extremely drought-tolerant and productive in hot summers where other beans struggle.
harvest_instructions: For fresh shell peas, pick when pods are plump and starting to change color. For dried peas, leave on the plant until pods are dry and crisp.
storage_instructions: Shell and refrigerate fresh peas for up to a week. Blanch and freeze for longer storage. Dried peas keep for years in sealed containers.
growing_tips: Thrives in heat and poor soil where many other crops fail
Wait until soil is thoroughly warm (70°F+) before planting
Excellent cover crop for building soil fertility through nitrogen fixation

description: Unique legume whose flowers send pegs into the soil where the nuts develop underground. A fun, educational plant to grow with children, producing surprisingly well even in cooler zones.
harvest_instructions: Harvest when foliage yellows and dies back, usually after first light frost. Dig the entire plant and shake off soil. Cure in a warm, dry place for 2-4 weeks.
storage_instructions: After curing, store in-shell peanuts in a cool, dry place for months. Shelled raw peanuts can be refrigerated or frozen for longer storage.
growing_tips: Choose short-season Valencia types for northern gardens
Loose, sandy soil is ideal; peanut pegs must be able to penetrate the ground
Hill soil around plants as they grow, similar to potatoes, to cover developing pegs

description: Italian green with a pleasantly bitter, nutty flavor, harvested for its tender stems, leaves, and small broccoli-like florets. A cornerstone of Italian-American cuisine.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems when buds are tight and just beginning to show yellow. Take 6-8 inch stems with attached leaves. Side shoots will continue to produce.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a plastic bag for up to 5 days. Blanch and freeze for longer storage.
growing_tips: Grows best in cool weather; bolts rapidly in heat
Succession plant every two weeks in spring and again in late summer for fall
Harvest promptly as the window between budding and flowering is short

description: Tall, barrel-shaped head of crinkled, pale green leaves with broad white ribs. Mild, sweet flavor makes it essential for kimchi, stir-fries, and Asian soups.
harvest_instructions: Cut the entire head at the base when it feels firm and full. Can also harvest outer leaves individually.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate whole heads in a plastic bag for up to 2 weeks. Keeps longer than regular cabbage.
growing_tips: Fall planting is more reliable than spring, as long days and heat cause bolting
Direct sow in midsummer for fall harvest rather than transplanting
Use row cover to protect from flea beetles, which heavily target napa cabbage

description: Elegant cabbage variety with deeply crinkled, tender leaves and a milder, sweeter flavor than smooth-leaved types. Beautiful dark blue-green heads hold up well in the garden.
harvest_instructions: Cut the head at the base when it is firm and has reached desired size. Outer leaves can be removed individually for use as wraps.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a perforated bag for up to 2 weeks. Does not store as long as smooth-leaved cabbage.
growing_tips: More cold-tolerant than standard cabbage; flavor sweetens after frost
Crinkled leaves are less attractive to cabbage worms than smooth-leaved varieties
Excellent for stuffed cabbage rolls due to pliable, tender leaves

description: Stunning brassica with a lime-green, fractal spiral head that is one of the most visually striking vegetables in the garden. Flavor combines nuttiness of broccoli with mildness of cauliflower.
harvest_instructions: Cut the head with a few inches of stem when the spirals are tight and well-formed, before they begin to separate. Typically 4-7 inches in diameter.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a plastic bag for up to 1 week. Can be blanched and frozen.
growing_tips: More temperamental than broccoli or cauliflower; needs consistent temperatures to form proper heads
Fall planting often produces better results than spring
Ensure steady, even watering and avoid any stress to prevent buttoning or misshapen heads

description: Gourmet allium with a delicate, complex flavor combining sweet onion and mild garlic notes. Each planted bulb multiplies to produce a cluster of smaller bulbs.
harvest_instructions: Harvest when the tops yellow and fall over. Gently dig up the clusters and cure in a warm, dry, well-ventilated area for 2-4 weeks.
storage_instructions: Store cured shallots in a cool, dry place in mesh bags or baskets. Keeps for 6-8 months with good air circulation.
growing_tips: Plant sets in fall for the largest bulbs, or early spring for a smaller but quicker harvest
Mulch fall-planted shallots well in zones 5 and below
Stop watering when tops begin to yellow to promote curing

description: Versatile allium harvested for its slender green tops and mild white base. One of the most frequently used garnishes in world cuisines, and one of the easiest alliums to grow.
harvest_instructions: Pull the entire plant when the base is pencil-thick, or cut the green tops and leave the plant to regrow. Perennial types can be harvested repeatedly.
storage_instructions: Stand in a jar of water on the counter for a few days, or refrigerate in a damp paper towel inside a plastic bag for up to 2 weeks.
growing_tips: Among the easiest and fastest alliums to grow from seed
Succession plant every 2 weeks for a continuous supply
Perennial bunching types like Evergreen Hardy White return year after year

description: Attractive perennial herb with flat, garlic-flavored leaves and edible white flower clusters. Used extensively in Asian cooking and as an ornamental border plant.
harvest_instructions: Cut leaves 2 inches above the base at any time during the growing season. Remove flower stalks to encourage more leaf production, or let them bloom for edible flowers.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a damp paper towel inside a plastic bag for up to 1 week. Freeze chopped chives for longer storage.
growing_tips: Deadhead spent flowers aggressively as garlic chives self-seed prolifically and can become invasive
Divide clumps every 3-4 years to maintain vigor
Both leaves and flowers are edible with a mild garlic flavor

description: Fascinating perennial onion that produces clusters of small bulbils at the top of its stalks, which bend over and 'walk' across the garden to plant themselves. All parts are edible.
harvest_instructions: Harvest green tops like scallions year-round. Pick top-set bulbils when they form in summer. Dig underground bulbs in fall. All parts have a strong onion flavor.
storage_instructions: Use greens fresh. Bulbils and underground bulbs can be stored in a mesh bag in a cool, dry place for several weeks.
growing_tips: Propagated by planting the top-set bulbils or dividing underground bulbs, not from seed
Extremely cold-hardy and virtually indestructible once established
Manage spread by harvesting bulbils before stalks fall over and self-plant

description: Tropical vine that produces long gourds edible when young and tender, or dried at maturity to create the familiar bath sponges. Vigorous climber needing a sturdy trellis.
harvest_instructions: For eating, harvest when 6-8 inches long and the skin is still tender. For sponges, leave on the vine until the skin turns brown and dry; peel and remove seeds.
storage_instructions: Eat young luffa within a few days. Dried sponges store indefinitely in a dry location.

companions.name: Nasturtium
companions.notes: Lures aphids and pests away from vegetables

growing_tips: Needs a long, hot season of at least 150 days; start indoors in cooler zones
Provide a very strong trellis or arbor for the heavy vines
Soak seeds overnight before planting to improve germination

description: Tropical vine producing distinctive warty, elongated fruits with an intensely bitter flavor valued in Asian and Indian cuisine. Known for its blood-sugar-lowering properties.
harvest_instructions: Harvest when fruits are still green and firm, 4-8 inches long. Do not wait until they turn yellow-orange as they become extremely bitter and the red seed coating is mildly toxic.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a paper bag for up to 5 days. Does not freeze well.
growing_tips: Needs warm soil and air temperatures to thrive; do not rush transplanting
Provide a sturdy trellis for climbing; fruits hang straight when trellised
Soak or scarify seeds before planting to speed germination

description: Vigorous tropical vine producing pear-shaped, light green fruits with a mild, crisp texture similar to zucchini. The entire plant is edible including fruit, shoots, leaves, and tuberous root.
harvest_instructions: Pick fruits when 4-6 inches long and still tender. The single large seed inside sprouts easily, so use the fruit promptly. Regular harvesting encourages more production.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a plastic bag for up to 3 weeks. Can be sliced and frozen.
growing_tips: Plant the whole sprouted fruit on its side with the stem end slightly exposed
Needs a very sturdy support structure; a single vine can cover a large arbor
In zones 7-8, mulch the crown heavily in winter; perennial root may survive mild winters

description: Actually a type of melon, this long, ribbed, pale green fruit is used like a cucumber but never develops bitterness. Heat-tolerant and productive when regular cucumbers struggle.
harvest_instructions: Harvest when 12-18 inches long and about 2 inches in diameter. Do not let them grow too large or the skin toughens. Pick frequently to encourage continued production.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a plastic bag for up to 1 week. Best eaten fresh as they do not store as long as regular cucumbers.

companions.name: Nasturtium
companions.notes: Lures aphids and pests away from vegetables

growing_tips: Trellising produces the straightest fruits and saves garden space
More heat-tolerant than regular cucumbers and never turns bitter
Technically a melon, so avoid planting near other melons to prevent cross-pollination if saving seeds

description: Heat-loving, vigorous climbing bean producing remarkably long, slender pods up to 18-30 inches. A staple in Southeast Asian stir-fries and curries, thriving in hot, humid summers.
harvest_instructions: Pick pods when 12-18 inches long, before the seeds inside begin to bulge. Harvest every 2-3 days for continuous production.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a plastic bag for up to 5 days. Blanch and freeze for longer storage. Cut into manageable lengths before cooking.

companions.name: Cucumber
companions.notes: Beans fix nitrogen for heavy-feeding cucumbers

antagonists.name: Onion
antagonists.notes: Alliums inhibit bean growth

antagonists.name: Garlic
antagonists.notes: Garlic stunts bean growth

antagonists.name: Fennel
antagonists.notes: Fennel releases compounds that inhibit most plant growth

growing_tips: Needs warm soil (70°F+) to germinate well; do not plant too early
Provide a tall trellis or pole at least 8 feet high
Produces best in high heat when standard green beans often struggle

description: Tropical legume with distinctive four-winged pods, every part of which is edible: pods, leaves, flowers, and tuberous roots. A nutritional powerhouse from Southeast Asia.
harvest_instructions: Pick pods when 6-8 inches long and still tender. Flowers can be harvested as they appear. Tubers are harvested at end of season like potatoes.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate young pods for up to 3 days. Seeds can be dried for storage. Tubers store like potatoes in a cool, dark place.

antagonists.name: Onion
antagonists.notes: Alliums inhibit bean growth

antagonists.name: Garlic
antagonists.notes: Garlic stunts bean growth

growing_tips: Requires consistently warm temperatures above 75°F; best in tropical or subtropical zones
Short day length triggers flowering; supplemental lighting may be needed in northern areas
Soak seeds for 24 hours before planting to improve germination

description: Heat-loving tropical vine with thick, glossy, succulent leaves used as a spinach substitute in hot weather. Available in green and attractive red-stemmed varieties.
harvest_instructions: Pick individual leaves and tender shoot tips regularly. The more you harvest, the bushier the plant grows. Leaves have a mild, slightly mucilaginous texture.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a damp paper towel for 3-5 days. Does not freeze well due to its succulent texture.
growing_tips: Thrives in heat and humidity where true spinach bolts
Provide a trellis for the twining vine, or let it sprawl as a ground cover
Soak or scarify seeds before planting as the hard seed coat slows germination

description: Heat-tolerant, sprawling ground cover plant with small, triangular, fleshy leaves used as a spinach substitute. Produces abundantly through the hottest months when true spinach has long bolted.
harvest_instructions: Pinch off the tender tips and young leaves, taking 3-4 inches of stem. Regular harvesting keeps the plant producing new growth all season.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a damp paper towel for up to 3 days. Best used fresh. Blanch before eating to remove oxalic acid.
growing_tips: Soak seeds overnight before planting to improve slow, erratic germination
Excellent ground cover that suppresses weeds once established
Not related to true spinach but makes a good warm-weather substitute

description: Succulent, low-growing plant often found as a garden weed but increasingly cultivated for its lemony, crunchy leaves. One of the richest plant sources of omega-3 fatty acids.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems and leaves when plants are 4-6 inches long. Cut-and-come-again approach works well. Harvest in the morning for the best flavor.
storage_instructions: Refrigerate in a damp paper towel for up to 3-5 days. Excellent pickled.
growing_tips: Seeds need light to germinate; scatter on the surface and press gently
Cultivated varieties are bushier and more upright than wild purslane
Extremely drought-tolerant due to succulent leaves; avoid overwatering

description: Pungent Mexican herb with a distinctive, complex aroma used traditionally with black beans and quesadillas. Also reputed to reduce the gas-producing compounds in beans.
harvest_instructions: Pick individual leaves and tender branch tips as needed throughout the growing season. Use sparingly as the flavor is very strong.
storage_instructions: Use fresh for best flavor. Can be dried and stored in an airtight container for several months.
growing_tips: Self-seeds aggressively; deadhead to prevent unwanted spread
Thrives in poor soil and requires very little care once established
Use sparingly in cooking; large quantities can be toxic

description: Aromatic Japanese herb with large, ruffled leaves in green or deep purple varieties. Complex flavor combines notes of basil, mint, and cinnamon. Essential in sushi, sashimi, and pickles.
harvest_instructions: Pick individual leaves as needed. Pinch off flower buds to encourage continued leaf production. Flower spikes and seeds are also edible.
storage_instructions: Wrap fresh leaves in damp paper towels and refrigerate for up to 1 week. Does not dry well; freeze in ice cube trays for longer storage.
growing_tips: Seeds need light and cold stratification for best germination; refrigerate for a week before sowing
Self-seeds readily; can become weedy if flowers are not removed
Purple varieties make a beautiful ornamental addition to borders and containers

description: Aromatic basil variety with purple stems, narrow leaves, and a distinctive anise-licorice flavor. Essential in Thai, Vietnamese, and other Southeast Asian cuisines.
harvest_instructions: Pinch or cut stems just above a leaf node to encourage bushy growth. Harvest regularly to delay flowering. Flowers are also edible.
storage_instructions: Stand stems in a glass of water at room temperature for several days. Refrigeration can cause blackening. Freeze in olive oil for longer storage.
growing_tips: More heat-tolerant and slower to bolt than sweet basil varieties
Pinch off flower buds regularly, though the purple blooms are attractive and edible
Pairs beautifully with other Southeast Asian garden crops like lemongrass and hot peppers

description: Intensely lemon-scented deciduous shrub from South America. Produces the most powerfully lemony fragrance of any herb, prized for teas, desserts, and potpourri.
harvest_instructions: Pick individual leaves at any time or cut stems back by one-third. Best flavor when harvested just before the plant flowers.
storage_instructions: Dry leaves retain their intense lemon fragrance for over a year. Store dried leaves in airtight containers away from light.
growing_tips: Grow in containers in zones below 8 and overwinter indoors; it will drop its leaves but recovers
Prune regularly to prevent legginess and encourage bushy growth
Dried leaves retain more fragrance than almost any other herb

description: Semi-evergreen herb with spikes of vivid blue, pink, or white flowers and a minty, slightly bitter flavor. Outstanding pollinator plant and traditional medicinal herb.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems just as flowers begin to open for the most potent essential oils. Cut back to about 6 inches above the base.
storage_instructions: Hang bundles to dry in a warm, airy place. Store dried hyssop in airtight containers for up to a year.
growing_tips: Excellent companion for grapes and brassicas, said to deter cabbage moths
Prefers well-drained, alkaline soil and tolerates drought once established
Cut back by one-third in late winter to maintain a compact, productive form

description: Tall, imposing perennial herb with a powerful celery-like flavor. All parts are edible: leaves, stems, seeds, and roots. One plant provides more than enough for most households.
harvest_instructions: Pick individual leaves throughout the season. Harvest hollow stems for use as straws or cooking tubes. Seeds can be collected when brown.
storage_instructions: Freeze chopped leaves in ice cube trays. Dry leaves lose much of their flavor. Seeds can be dried and stored in airtight containers.
growing_tips: One plant is usually sufficient as lovage grows very large and has an intense flavor
Cut back flower stalks if you want to promote continued leaf production
Mature plants can be divided in spring or fall to propagate

description: Delicate French herb with lacy, fern-like leaves and a subtle anise-parsley flavor. One of the classic fines herbes of French cuisine, best used fresh as cooking destroys its flavor.
harvest_instructions: Snip individual leaves or cut the entire plant 1-2 inches above the base. Best used fresh; add at the end of cooking to preserve its delicate flavor.
storage_instructions: Wrap in damp paper towels and refrigerate for up to 1 week. Freezing preserves flavor better than drying.
growing_tips: Prefers cool weather and partial shade; bolts rapidly in heat and sun
Does not transplant well; direct sow and thin
Sow in fall for an early spring harvest; seeds need cold to germinate well

description: Delicately flavored annual herb with a peppery, thyme-like taste traditionally paired with beans. Milder and sweeter than its perennial cousin, winter savory.
harvest_instructions: Snip stems just before or during flowering for the most aromatic leaves. Cut the entire plant when harvesting for drying.
storage_instructions: Dry stems by hanging in bundles. Dried summer savory keeps its flavor well for 6 months in airtight containers.
growing_tips: Known as the 'bean herb' for its classic pairing with all types of beans
Plants can become leggy; pinch growing tips to encourage bushiness
Prefers lean, well-drained soil; rich soil produces less flavorful leaves

description: Woody, evergreen perennial herb with a strong, peppery, resinous flavor more intense than summer savory. Used in hearty winter dishes, sausages, and with dried beans.
harvest_instructions: Snip sprigs as needed throughout the growing season. Do a larger harvest before flowering for drying. Avoid cutting back more than one-third at a time.
storage_instructions: Hang sprigs to dry or lay flat on a screen. Store dried leaves in airtight containers for up to a year.
growing_tips: Grows slowly from seed; cuttings or divisions are faster for propagation
Excellent as a low, evergreen border or edging plant
Well-drained soil is essential; crown rot occurs in wet winter soil

description: Hardy perennial herb famous for its euphoric effect on cats. Also used to make a mild, calming herbal tea and as a mosquito repellent. Produces spikes of white to lavender flowers.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems just as flowers begin to open. Harvest in the morning after dew dries for the highest essential oil content.
storage_instructions: Hang bundles to dry in a warm, airy place. Store dried catnip in sealed containers away from feline access.
growing_tips: If growing for cats, protect young plants until established or cats will destroy them
Spreads readily by self-seeding; deadhead to control spread
Contains nepetalactone, a natural insect repellent effective against mosquitoes and aphids

description: Showy perennial herb also known as bee balm or Oswego tea, with vibrant red, pink, or purple tubular flowers that attract hummingbirds and pollinators. Leaves make a citrusy, aromatic tea.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems when flowers are at their peak for fresh or dried use. Harvest leaves anytime for tea. Flowers are also edible and make a beautiful garnish.
storage_instructions: Dry leaves and flowers by hanging bundles in a cool, dark, well-ventilated area. Store in airtight containers for up to a year.
growing_tips: Prone to powdery mildew; ensure good air circulation and choose resistant varieties
Divide clumps every 3 years as the centers die out
Seeds need light to germinate; surface sow and do not cover

description: Majestic, architectural herb growing up to 6 feet tall with large, compound leaves and dramatic spherical flower heads. All parts are aromatic, with flavors of celery, anise, and juniper.
harvest_instructions: Harvest leaves in the first year. Cut stems in spring of the second year before flowering for candying. Dig roots in fall of the first year for the most potent flavor.
storage_instructions: Use leaves fresh or freeze. Stems can be candied and stored in sugar for months. Dry roots and store in airtight containers for up to a year.
growing_tips: Seeds must be fresh and require cold stratification; sow in fall for spring germination
Prefers cool, moist, partly shaded conditions; mimick its native woodland edge habitat
Dies after flowering in the second year but self-seeds if allowed to go to seed

description: A native North American wildflower prized for its striking purple-pink daisy-like blooms and prominent spiny central cone. Extremely drought-tolerant once established and highly attractive to pollinators and butterflies.
harvest_instructions: Cut flowers when petals are fully open. Harvest seed heads when the central cone turns dark brown and seeds are loose. Leave some seed heads standing for winter bird food.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut flowers last 7-10 days in water. Dried seed heads can be stored in paper bags in a cool, dry place for seed saving.
growing_tips: Seeds require cold stratification for 4-6 weeks before spring sowing to improve germination.
Do not deadhead all spent blooms; leave some for self-seeding and wildlife habitat.
Divide mature clumps every 3-4 years in spring to maintain vigor.
Avoid overwatering; coneflowers are native prairie plants that thrive in lean, well-drained soils.

description: A compact, heavy-blooming cultivar of black-eyed Susan producing masses of golden-yellow, daisy-like flowers with dark chocolate-brown centers from midsummer through fall. An exceptionally reliable and low-maintenance garden perennial.
harvest_instructions: Cut flowers when petals are fully reflexed and the central cone is prominent. Harvest seed heads when they turn dark and dry on the stem.
storage_instructions: Fresh flowers last 6-10 days in a vase. Dried seed heads can be stored in envelopes in a cool, dry location.
growing_tips: Divide clumps every 3-4 years in early spring to prevent overcrowding and maintain bloom quality.
Tolerates clay soil better than most perennials but still prefers good drainage.
Leave spent flower heads through winter for bird food and architectural interest.
Seeds need light to germinate; do not cover with soil when sowing.

description: A stately woodland biennial producing towering spikes of tubular, spotted flowers in shades of purple, pink, white, and yellow during its second year. All parts of the plant are poisonous and should not be planted near edible crops.
harvest_instructions: Cut flower spikes when the lowest one-third of blooms are open for the longest vase life. Wear gloves when handling as all parts are toxic.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut spikes last 5-7 days in water. Allow some stalks to remain for self-seeding to maintain a colony year after year.
growing_tips: Seeds are very fine and need light to germinate; press gently onto the soil surface without covering.
As a biennial, it forms a rosette of leaves the first year and flowers the second; allow self-seeding for continuous yearly blooms.
Prefers humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in partial shade, mimicking woodland edge conditions.
All plant parts are highly toxic if ingested. Keep away from children and pets.

description: A showy perennial producing tall, dense spikes of pea-like flowers in a wide range of colors including blue, purple, pink, red, yellow, and bicolors. As a legume, it fixes nitrogen in the soil, benefiting neighboring plants.
harvest_instructions: Cut flower spikes when one-quarter to one-half of the blooms are open. Deadhead spent flowers to encourage a second, smaller flush of blooms.
storage_instructions: Fresh flowers last 5-7 days in water. Seed pods can be harvested when they turn brown and begin to split, then stored in a cool, dry place.
growing_tips: Scarify or nick the hard seed coat and soak seeds overnight before planting to improve germination.
Prefers cool summers; struggles in hot, humid climates above zone 7.
Lupines fix nitrogen through root nodules, making them excellent companions for nutrient-hungry plants.
Provide well-drained, slightly acidic soil; avoid lime and alkaline conditions.

description: A late-season native perennial that produces an abundance of small, daisy-like flowers in shades of purple, blue, pink, and white, providing essential nectar for migrating butterflies and bees when few other flowers remain in bloom.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems when about half the flowers on a spray are open. Strip lower leaves before placing in water for the best vase life.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut asters last 5-10 days in water with a floral preservative. Change water every 2 days.
growing_tips: Pinch stems back by half in early June to promote bushier growth and prevent flopping.
Divide clumps every 2-3 years in spring to maintain vigor and prevent center die-out.
Seeds need cold stratification for 4-6 weeks to germinate; fall sowing outdoors works well.
Provide good air circulation to reduce susceptibility to powdery mildew.

description: The quintessential fall-blooming perennial offering an enormous range of flower forms and colors. Mums are photoperiod-sensitive, blooming as days shorten, and are one of the last flowers to provide color before winter.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems when flowers are nearly fully open. Harvest in the morning and immediately place in cool water to maximize vase life.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut mums last 2-3 weeks in clean water with floral preservative. Store potted mums in a cool, bright location.
growing_tips: Pinch growing tips every 2-3 weeks from spring through early July to promote bushy, compact growth with more blooms.
Plant in spring (not fall) to allow roots to establish before winter for reliable perennial performance.
Apply winter mulch after the ground freezes to prevent heaving in cold climates.
Avoid overhead watering to reduce foliar disease; water at the base of the plant.

description: A classic fragrant flower with frilly, ruffled petals in shades of pink, red, white, and yellow, long prized for cut-flower arrangements and garden borders. Its spicy-sweet clove scent is distinctive among garden flowers.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems when blooms are half to three-quarters open. Cut at an angle just above a leaf node to encourage reblooming.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut carnations can last up to 3 weeks in clean water with floral preservative. One of the longest-lasting fresh-cut flowers available.
growing_tips: Provide excellent drainage; carnations are susceptible to root rot in heavy, wet soils.
Prefers slightly alkaline soil; amend acidic soils with garden lime.
Disbud side shoots to produce larger individual blooms for cutting.
Stake tall varieties to prevent stems from snapping in wind or rain.

description: Majestic bulbous perennials producing large, showy, often fragrant trumpet or star-shaped flowers on tall sturdy stems. Asiatic, Oriental, and trumpet types offer a succession of bloom from early to late summer.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems when the first bud on the stem is just beginning to open and shows color. Remove anthers to prevent pollen stains on petals and furniture.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut lilies last 7-14 days in water. Unplanted bulbs should be stored in slightly moist peat moss in a cool (35-40F) location.
growing_tips: Plant bulbs in fall at a depth of 3 times the bulb's height, with the pointed end facing up.
Provide well-drained soil; bulbs will rot in waterlogged conditions.
Mulch over bulbs to keep roots cool and retain moisture during summer heat.
The red lily beetle is a serious pest; inspect plants regularly and remove beetles and larvae by hand.

description: Iconic spring-blooming bulbs producing cup-shaped flowers in nearly every color imaginable. While technically perennial, most hybrid tulips perform best when treated as annuals and replanted each fall for reliable spring color.
harvest_instructions: Cut when buds are colored but still closed; they will open fully in a vase. Pull rather than cut for the longest possible stem.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut tulips last 5-7 days in cool water. Store unplanted bulbs in mesh bags in a cool (50-65F), dry location until fall planting.
growing_tips: Plant bulbs in mid to late fall, 6-8 inches deep and pointed end up, after soil temperatures drop below 60F.
Species tulips (T. tarda, T. clusiana) perennialize more reliably than hybrid types.
Allow foliage to die back naturally after blooming; the leaves recharge the bulb for the following year.
In zones 8+, pre-chill bulbs in the refrigerator for 12-14 weeks before planting to simulate winter.

description: Cheerful spring-blooming bulbs bearing trumpet-shaped flowers in shades of yellow, white, and orange. Daffodils are deer- and rodent-resistant due to toxic alkaloids in all plant parts, making them among the most reliable spring bulbs.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems when buds are in the 'goose-neck' stage, just as they bend downward and show color but before opening. Sap from cut stems can harm other flowers, so condition daffodils alone in water for several hours before mixing with other blooms.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut daffodils last 5-10 days in water. Store dormant bulbs in mesh bags in a cool, dry, well-ventilated location.
growing_tips: Plant bulbs in early to mid fall, 6 inches deep with the pointed end up, before the ground freezes.
Naturalizes readily; leave undisturbed clumps to multiply for years of increasing spring displays.
Do not braid, tie, or mow foliage after blooming; allow leaves to yellow naturally over 6 weeks.
All parts are toxic if ingested; the alkaloids also make them completely deer- and vole-proof.

description: Among the earliest spring bloomers, these small corms produce cheerful goblet-shaped flowers in purple, white, yellow, and striped varieties, often pushing up through late-season snow. Excellent for naturalizing in lawns and rock gardens.
harvest_instructions: Crocus are rarely cut but can be picked when buds show color. They close at night and in cloudy weather, opening again in sunlight.
storage_instructions: Leave corms in the ground year-round for naturalizing. If lifting, store dried corms in mesh bags in a cool, dry spot.
growing_tips: Plant corms 3-4 inches deep in early to mid fall before the ground freezes.
Excellent for naturalizing in lawns; delay mowing in spring until crocus foliage has yellowed.
Plant in clusters of 10-25 for the best visual impact rather than single rows.
Squirrels may dig corms; cover newly planted areas with wire mesh until settled.

description: A fragrant spring bulb bearing dense, columnar spikes of waxy, bell-shaped florets in blue, pink, white, purple, and yellow. One of the most intensely fragrant spring flowers, a single spike can perfume an entire room.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems when about one-third of the florets on the spike are open. Pull the entire stem from the bulb rather than cutting for maximum length.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut hyacinths last 7-10 days in water. Store dormant bulbs in a cool, dry, well-ventilated location. Wear gloves when handling as bulbs contain oxalic acid that irritates skin.
growing_tips: Plant bulbs in mid to late fall, 5-6 inches deep with the pointed end up.
Flower spikes become looser and more graceful after the first year; many gardeners prefer the naturalized look.
Wear gloves when handling bulbs as they contain compounds that cause contact dermatitis.
In zones 8+, pre-chill bulbs in the refrigerator for 10-12 weeks before planting.

description: Elegant bearded irises produce large, ruffled flowers with distinctive upright 'standards' and drooping 'falls' in a vast palette of colors. Grown from rhizomes, they are among the most striking and easy-care late-spring perennials.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems when the first bud on a stalk is just beginning to open and shows color. Each stem will continue to open successive buds in the vase.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut irises last 3-5 days in water. Store bare rhizomes in a dry, airy location out of direct sun until planting.
growing_tips: Plant rhizomes in late summer with the top of the rhizome exposed at or just below the soil surface; burying too deep invites rot.
Divide overcrowded clumps every 3-4 years in late summer, discarding the old center section and replanting the outer fans.
Good air circulation and avoiding mulch directly over rhizomes helps prevent iris borer and bacterial soft rot.
Remove and destroy any leaves showing iris borer tunneling damage to break the pest cycle.

description: Dramatic ornamental onion relatives that produce large, perfectly spherical heads of tiny star-shaped flowers atop tall, leafless stems. Their architectural form adds vertical interest and the dried seed heads persist beautifully into fall and winter.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems when about three-quarters of the tiny florets in the head are open. Dried seed heads can be harvested after blooming for everlasting arrangements.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut alliums last 7-14 days in water. Dried seed heads last indefinitely. Store unplanted bulbs in a cool, dry location.
growing_tips: Plant bulbs in fall, 6-8 inches deep, in well-drained soil; bulbs rot in wet winter soils.
Foliage often yellows and dies back as the flower opens; interplant with perennials that will hide the declining leaves.
Deer, rabbits, and rodents avoid alliums due to their onion scent and taste.
Leave dried seed heads standing for architectural winter interest and self-seeding.

description: Delicate, papery-petaled flowers in vivid shades of red, orange, pink, and white that dance in the slightest breeze. Annual poppies are among the easiest wildflowers to grow from direct-sown seed and self-sow prolifically.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems early in the morning when buds are just cracking open and showing color. Sear the cut end of the stem with a flame or dip in boiling water to prevent sap from sealing the stem and blocking water uptake.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut poppies last 3-5 days if stems are properly seared. Seed pods dry beautifully for everlasting arrangements.
growing_tips: Direct sow in late fall or very early spring; seeds need cold to germinate and do not transplant well.
Mix tiny seeds with sand for more even distribution when broadcasting over the soil surface.
Seeds need light to germinate; press onto soil surface but do not cover.
Allow seed pods to dry on the plant and shake seeds for next year's self-sown display.

description: An old-fashioned cottage garden annual producing tall spikes of spurted flowers in blue, pink, purple, and white that resemble its perennial cousin, delphinium. Much easier to grow than delphinium and thrives in cool spring weather.
harvest_instructions: Cut spikes when the lower one-third of flowers are open and the upper buds are still tight. All parts are toxic if ingested.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut larkspur lasts 5-7 days in water. Dries exceptionally well; hang upside down in a dark, dry, warm location for 2-3 weeks.
growing_tips: Direct sow in fall or very early spring; seeds require darkness and cool temperatures (55F) to germinate.
Refrigerate seeds for 2 weeks before spring sowing to simulate winter chill and improve germination.
Does not transplant well due to a taproot; direct sowing is strongly preferred.
All parts are toxic if ingested; wear gloves when handling.

description: Low-growing, spreading plants producing clusters of small, brightly colored flowers in shades of red, purple, pink, white, and bicolors from late spring through frost. Excellent heat and drought tolerance make them ideal for containers and borders.
harvest_instructions: Deadhead spent flower clusters regularly to promote continuous blooming throughout the season.
storage_instructions: Not typically harvested for storage. Take stem cuttings in late summer to overwinter indoors in cold climates.
growing_tips: Seeds require darkness to germinate; cover completely after sowing and keep at 65-75F.
Shear plants back by one-third in midsummer if they become leggy to promote fresh growth and renewed blooming.
Excellent powdery mildew resistance compared to older varieties; ensure good air circulation nonetheless.
Thrives in heat and tolerates drought once established, making it a good choice for hot, dry locations.

description: Tropical annuals with vivid, velvety flower heads in crested (cockscomb), plumed, or spiked forms in brilliant shades of red, orange, yellow, and pink. Heat-loving plants that intensify in color as summer progresses.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems when flower heads are fully colored and developed. Strip lower foliage and place immediately in water.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut celosia lasts 7-14 days in water. Dries exceptionally well; hang upside down in bundles for 2-3 weeks and it retains its vivid color.

companions.name: Marigold
companions.notes: Repels nematodes, aphids, and many garden pests

growing_tips: Very frost-sensitive; do not transplant outdoors until nighttime temperatures are consistently above 55F.
Avoid disturbing roots when transplanting; becoming root-bound or stressed causes premature blooming at a small size.
Thrives in hot weather when many other annuals falter; an excellent choice for southern and tropical gardens.
Seeds need light to germinate; sow on the surface of moist potting mix.

description: A dramatic, tall annual producing long, cascading rope-like tassels of tiny flowers in deep crimson, gold, or green. Both ornamental and edible, the protein-rich seeds and tender young leaves have been cultivated as food for thousands of years.
harvest_instructions: For grain, harvest seed heads when they begin to shed seeds easily when rubbed. For greens, pick tender young leaves before flowering. Hang seed heads upside down over a tarp to dry and thresh.
storage_instructions: Dried seeds store for years in airtight containers in a cool, dry place. Young leaves can be blanched and frozen like spinach.
growing_tips: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm (65F+); seeds are very small, so mix with sand for even sowing.
Extremely heat- and drought-tolerant once established; an excellent crop for hot, dry climates.
Young leaves are edible and nutritious, similar to spinach; harvest before plants flower for the best flavor.
Can become weedy through self-seeding; remove spent flower heads if self-sowing is not desired.

description: A charming, old-fashioned annual wildflower producing frilly, button-shaped blooms in brilliant true blue, as well as pink, white, and purple. One of the few flowers that produce a genuinely blue color, and the petals are edible.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems when flowers are just fully open. Petals are edible and can be used fresh to garnish salads, desserts, and drinks.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut cornflowers last 5-7 days in water. Dries well for crafts; hang upside down in a dark, dry location for 2 weeks.
growing_tips: Direct sow in fall or very early spring for the strongest plants; they prefer cool growing conditions.
Does not transplant well; direct sowing is strongly preferred.
Deadhead spent blooms regularly to extend the flowering period well into summer.
Self-sows readily; allow some flowers to go to seed for a naturalized meadow effect.

description: Also known as spider flower, cleome produces airy, open clusters of spidery blooms with long, protruding stamens atop tall, sturdy stems. A hummingbird magnet that adds height and an exotic, tropical look to beds and borders.
harvest_instructions: Not typically used as a cut flower due to its thorny stems and musky scent. Best enjoyed in the garden.
storage_instructions: Allow seed pods to dry on the plant; collect elongated pods before they split open and scatter seeds.
growing_tips: Seeds benefit from cold stratification; chill for 1-2 weeks before sowing or fall-sow for spring germination.
Self-sows aggressively; deadhead to control spread or allow for a naturalized display.
Tolerates heat, drought, and poor soil once established.
Stems have small thorns; wear gloves when working around plants.

description: A bushy, fragrant plant that opens its trumpet-shaped flowers in late afternoon, hence the name. Blooms come in a dazzling array of colors including pink, red, yellow, white, and often multicolored or striped on the same plant.
harvest_instructions: Flowers open in late afternoon and last until the following morning. Not commonly used as cut flowers. Collect the large black seeds when they dry on the plant.
storage_instructions: Seeds store well in paper envelopes in a cool, dry place. In zones 9+, tuberous roots can be left in the ground year-round; in colder zones, dig and store like dahlias.
growing_tips: Nick or soak the hard seed coat overnight before planting to speed germination.
Perennial in zones 9-11 via a tuberous root; grown as an annual in colder regions or dig and store tubers.
Attracts sphinx moths and other nocturnal pollinators with its evening fragrance.
All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested; keep away from children and pets.

description: Ornamental flowering tobacco produces clusters of tubular, star-shaped flowers that release an intoxicatingly sweet fragrance in the evening. Available in white, green, pink, red, and purple, it is a magnet for hummingbirds and night-flying moths.
harvest_instructions: Not typically used as a cut flower. Enjoy in the garden for its evening fragrance. Remove spent flower stalks to encourage reblooming.
storage_instructions: Allow seed capsules to dry on the plant, then collect and store seeds in envelopes. Self-sows readily in mild climates.
growing_tips: Seeds are very fine and need light to germinate; sow on the surface and press gently without covering.
The taller species (N. sylvestris) are more fragrant than compact bedding varieties.
As a member of the Solanaceae family, avoid planting where tomatoes, peppers, or eggplant grew previously.
All parts are toxic if ingested; handle with care.

description: A richly fragrant annual with clusters of tiny purple, blue, or white flowers that emit a heavenly vanilla-cherry scent. Victorian gardeners prized heliotrope for its perfume, and it remains one of the most wonderfully fragrant bedding plants.
harvest_instructions: Cut flower clusters when most of the tiny florets are open. Enjoy the vanilla-like fragrance indoors.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut heliotrope lasts 5-7 days in water. Take stem cuttings in late summer to root and overwinter indoors.
growing_tips: Start seeds very early indoors (10-12 weeks before last frost) as germination is slow and erratic.
Seeds need light and consistent warmth (70-75F) to germinate; do not cover.
Pinch young plants to promote bushy growth and more flower clusters.
All parts are toxic if ingested; keep away from children and pets.

description: A cool-season annual producing dense spikes of clove-scented double flowers in soft pastels of pink, purple, white, cream, and red. Prized as a cut flower for its exceptional fragrance and cottage-garden charm.
harvest_instructions: Cut stems when about one-third of the flowers on the spike are open. Pull the entire plant if possible for the longest stem.
storage_instructions: Fresh-cut stock lasts 7-10 days in water with floral preservative. Change water frequently as stems can produce a sulfurous smell.
growing_tips: Prefers cool growing temperatures (50-65F); heat causes plants to bolt and stop flowering.
In mild winter climates (zones 8-10), grow as a winter/early spring flower for best results.
As a Brassicaceae family member, susceptible to clubroot; rotate planting locations annually.
Chill seeds at 40F for one week before sowing to improve double-flower percentage.

description: A stunning cover crop that fixes atmospheric nitrogen through symbiotic root bacteria while producing brilliant crimson-red flower spikes that attract beneficial insects. Among the most beautiful and hardworking cover crops available.
harvest_instructions: Mow or incorporate into soil at peak bloom (about 50% flowering) for maximum nitrogen fixation benefit. Can also be mowed and left as mulch.
storage_instructions: Store seed in a cool, dry place. Seed remains viable for 2-3 years under proper storage conditions.

companions.name: Oats (Cover Crop)
companions.notes: Fixes nitrogen in soil for following crops

companions.name: Annual Ryegrass
companions.notes: Fixes nitrogen in soil for following crops

companions.name: Winter Rye
companions.notes: Fixes nitrogen in soil for following crops

growing_tips: Sow in late summer to early fall (6-8 weeks before first frost) for winter cover; spring sowing is less common.
Fixes 70-150 lbs of nitrogen per acre when inoculated with the proper Rhizobium bacteria.
Mow or till in at peak bloom before seed set to prevent it from becoming a weed in subsequent crops.
Excellent bee forage; one of the most pollinator-friendly cover crops available.

description: A low-growing, creeping perennial legume widely used as a living mulch, ground cover, and nitrogen-fixing companion in orchards and garden pathways. Its white flower heads are an important nectar source for honeybees.
harvest_instructions: Not harvested. Mow periodically to maintain as a living mulch. Can be tilled in as a green manure when ready to plant the area.
storage_instructions: Store seed in a cool, dry, dark location. Clover seed remains viable for 3-5 years under good storage conditions.

companions.name: Fruit trees
companions.notes: Fixes nitrogen in soil for following crops

companions.name: Blueberry
companions.notes: Fixes nitrogen in soil for following crops

companions.name: Grape
companions.notes: Fixes nitrogen in soil for following crops

growing_tips: Broadcast seed onto prepared soil in spring or early fall and press or roll lightly; seeds need soil contact but not deep burial.
Fixes 75-200 lbs of nitrogen per acre annually through symbiotic root bacteria.
Excellent living mulch for orchards and perennial beds; spreads by stolons to form a dense, weed-suppressing mat.
Tolerates foot traffic and mowing; ideal for garden pathways between beds.

description: The hardiest of all cereal grain cover crops, winter rye germinates quickly in cold soil and produces a dense mat of roots that prevents erosion, suppresses weeds, and scavenges residual nitrogen. An essential tool for building soil health over winter.
harvest_instructions: Mow or crimp at boot stage (before seed heads emerge) for easiest incorporation. Allow 2-3 weeks of decomposition before planting cash crops into the residue.
storage_instructions: Store seed in a cool, dry location. Cereal rye seed keeps for 2-3 years with proper storage.

companions.name: Hairy Vetch
companions.notes: Suppresses weeds and prevents erosion

companions.name: Crimson Clover
companions.notes: Suppresses weeds and prevents erosion

companions.name: Austrian Winter Pea
companions.notes: Suppresses weeds and prevents erosion

growing_tips: Sow in early to late fall; can be planted later than most cover crops and still establish before winter.
The allelopathic root exudates suppress weed seed germination, making it an excellent natural weed control.
Terminate at least 2-3 weeks before planting spring crops; decomposing rye residue can temporarily inhibit germination of small-seeded crops.
Combines well with hairy vetch or crimson clover for a nitrogen-fixing grass-legume cover crop mix.

description: A fast-growing, warm-season cover crop that smothers weeds, attracts beneficial insects with its abundant white flowers, and makes phosphorus more available in the soil. Reaches full bloom in just 4-6 weeks, making it ideal for short gaps between crops.
harvest_instructions: Mow or till in at peak bloom (about 35-40 days after sowing) before seeds set. For grain harvest, cut when 75% of seeds are brown.
storage_instructions: Store seed in a cool, dry place. Buckwheat seed remains viable for 2-3 years. Harvested grain stores well in airtight containers.

companions.name: Crimson Clover
companions.notes: Attracts beneficial insects; smothers weeds

companions.name: Phacelia
companions.notes: Attracts beneficial insects; smothers weeds

growing_tips: Very frost-sensitive; do not sow until after the last frost date when soil temperatures reach 55F.
Killed by the first hard frost, leaving a weed-free mulch that breaks down easily over winter.
Attracts hoverflies, parasitic wasps, and other beneficial insects that prey on garden pests.
Excellent phosphorus scavenger; roots solubilize bound phosphorus and make it available for subsequent crops.

description: A vigorous, vining winter-hardy legume that is one of the most effective nitrogen-fixing cover crops, capable of producing 100-200 lbs of plant-available nitrogen per acre. Its dense, mat-forming growth makes it an exceptional weed suppressor.
harvest_instructions: Mow or crimp at peak bloom in late spring. The dense mat of killed vetch makes an excellent weed-suppressing mulch for no-till vegetable transplanting.
storage_instructions: Store seed in a cool, dry location. Inoculate with appropriate Rhizobium before planting if sowing in a field for the first time.

companions.name: Winter Rye
companions.notes: Fixes nitrogen and adds organic matter

companions.name: Oats (Cover Crop)
companions.notes: Fixes nitrogen and adds organic matter

companions.name: Crimson Clover
companions.notes: Fixes nitrogen and adds organic matter

growing_tips: Sow in late summer to early fall, ideally 4-6 weeks before the first expected frost.
Inoculate seed with the appropriate Vicia-group Rhizobium inoculant for maximum nitrogen fixation.
Combine with winter rye for a balanced cover crop mix: rye provides structure for vetch to climb.
Can self-seed aggressively if allowed to set seed; terminate before seed maturity to prevent volunteer issues.

description: A cool-season legume cover crop that fixes nitrogen and produces abundant biomass over winter. Its vining growth habit makes it an excellent companion when paired with upright cereal grains like oats or rye in a cover crop mix.
harvest_instructions: Mow or incorporate into soil at peak bloom for maximum nitrogen benefit. Can also be grazed by livestock.
storage_instructions: Store seed in a cool, dry place. Pea seed remains viable for 3-4 years under proper conditions.
growing_tips: Sow in early fall, 6-8 weeks before the first expected frost, for best winter survival.
Less winter-hardy than hairy vetch; may winter-kill in zones 5 and below without snow cover.
Fixes 50-150 lbs of nitrogen per acre; inoculate with pea/vetch-group Rhizobium for best results.
Excellent companion with oats or rye; the grain provides physical support for the vining peas.

description: A fast-establishing grass cover crop with a dense, fibrous root system that excels at erosion control and weed suppression. Quick to germinate and easy to manage, it is one of the most accessible cover crops for home gardeners.
harvest_instructions: Mow or till in before it sets seed in late spring. Can be mowed multiple times and left as mulch.
storage_instructions: Store seed in a cool, dry location. Annual ryegrass seed remains viable for 3-5 years.

companions.name: Crimson Clover
companions.notes: Suppresses weeds and prevents erosion

companions.name: Hairy Vetch
companions.notes: Suppresses weeds and prevents erosion

companions.name: Austrian Winter Pea
companions.notes: Suppresses weeds and prevents erosion

growing_tips: Sow in late summer or early fall for best establishment; can also be spring-sown.
Germinates quickly (5-7 days) and establishes faster than any other cover crop grass.
Be cautious: can become difficult to terminate in spring if allowed to mature; mow or till before seed set.
Excellent for improving soil structure through its dense, fibrous root system.

description: A fast-growing, cool-season cereal grain used as a nurse crop and cover crop that scavenges nutrients, suppresses weeds, and winter-kills in most climates, leaving an easy-to-manage residue for spring planting.
harvest_instructions: In most zones, oats will winter-kill and leave a residue that can be raked or tilled into soil in spring. For grain, harvest when kernels are hard and straw is golden.
storage_instructions: Store seed in a cool, dry place. Oat seed is viable for 3-4 years under good conditions.
growing_tips: Sow in late summer or early fall; oats will winter-kill in zones 6 and below, eliminating the need for spring termination.
An excellent nurse crop: sow alongside slower-establishing legume cover crops to provide quick ground cover.
Scavenges excess nitrogen from the soil, preventing it from leaching into groundwater over winter.
The winter-killed residue creates a natural mulch that protects soil and suppresses early spring weeds.

scientific_name: Sorghum bicolor x S. bicolor var. sudanense
description: A vigorous, heat-loving summer cover crop that produces massive amounts of organic matter and has deep roots that break up compacted soil layers. One of the most productive biomass-generating cover crops available for warm-season use.
harvest_instructions: Mow when 3-4 feet tall to promote regrowth and prevent lodging. It will regrow vigorously from the crown. Incorporate the final mowing before frost.
storage_instructions: Store seed in a cool, dry location. Seed remains viable for 3-5 years.

companions.name: Buckwheat
companions.notes: Attracts beneficial insects; smothers weeds

growing_tips: Requires warm soil (65F+) to germinate; do not plant until at least 4 weeks after the last frost.
Mow at 3-4 feet tall rather than letting it grow to full height; regrowth produces more biomass and is easier to manage.
Deep roots penetrate hardpan and compacted subsoil, improving drainage and soil structure for future crops.
Young shoots can contain toxic levels of prussic acid (hydrogen cyanide); do not graze or harvest for livestock feed when plants are under 18 inches tall.

description: A fast-growing cover crop and pollinator plant bearing coiled clusters of lavender-blue flowers that are among the most attractive to honeybees of any plant. Belongs to no major crop family, making it ideal for crop rotations.
harvest_instructions: Mow or incorporate into soil at peak bloom. Its soft, non-woody tissue breaks down rapidly.
storage_instructions: Store seed in a cool, dry place. Phacelia seed is viable for 2-3 years.

companions.name: Buckwheat
companions.notes: Attracts beneficial insects; smothers weeds

growing_tips: Belongs to no major vegetable crop family, making it uniquely valuable in crop rotation plans.
One of the top bee-attracting plants in the world; excellent for boosting pollination in adjacent food crops.
Frost-sensitive; use as a spring or summer cover crop. Winter-kills in zones 7 and below.
Seeds need darkness to germinate; cover lightly with soil after sowing.

description: A vigorous, multi-stemmed deciduous shrub producing large, flat clusters of fragrant white flowers followed by dense bunches of small, dark purple-black berries. Berries must be cooked before eating and are traditionally used for syrups, wines, and immune-supporting preparations.
harvest_instructions: Harvest entire berry clusters when most berries are deep purple-black and slightly soft. Cut clusters with pruners. Strip berries from stems using a fork. Do NOT eat raw; cook before consuming.
storage_instructions: Fresh berries last 1-2 days refrigerated. Freeze on sheet trays, then strip from stems. Cooked syrup, juice, or jam stores well canned or frozen.
growing_tips: Plant two different cultivars for cross-pollination and maximum berry production.
Prune out canes older than 3 years in late winter to maintain productive, vigorous growth.
Raw berries, leaves, and stems contain cyanogenic glycosides and must be cooked before consumption.
Thrives in moist, rich soil; tolerates poorly drained conditions better than most fruit crops.

description: A fast-growing, long-lived deciduous tree producing prolific quantities of sweet, elongated blackberry-like fruits in early summer. Extremely low-maintenance once established and attractive to a wide variety of birds and wildlife.
harvest_instructions: Spread a tarp or sheet beneath the tree and shake branches; ripe berries fall easily. Berries can also be picked by hand. They ripen over several weeks, allowing multiple harvests.
storage_instructions: Very perishable; use within 1-2 days fresh. Freeze on sheet trays for long-term storage. Excellent dried, in jams, or in baked goods.

companions.name: Nasturtium
companions.notes: Lures aphids and pests away from vegetables

growing_tips: Choose planting location carefully: falling fruit stains surfaces, so avoid planting near walkways, driveways, and patios.
Select named cultivars grafted onto rootstock for reliable fruit quality; seedlings are highly variable.
Prune in late winter to maintain a manageable size and improve air circulation.
One of the lowest-maintenance fruit-producing trees; rarely troubled by serious pests or diseases.

description: A beautiful multi-season shrub or small tree offering clouds of white spring flowers, sweet blueberry-like fruits in June, and brilliant fall foliage. One of the most ornamental and productive native edible landscape plants available.
harvest_instructions: Pick berries when they turn deep purple-blue and are soft and sweet. Harvest quickly as birds compete aggressively for the fruit. Netting may be necessary.
storage_instructions: Fresh berries last 3-5 days refrigerated. Freeze for long-term storage. Use like blueberries in pies, jams, sauces, and smoothies.
growing_tips: Adapts to a wide range of soils but prefers moist, well-drained, slightly acidic conditions.
Bird netting is often necessary to harvest berries before birds strip the shrub clean.
Minimal pruning required; remove dead, crossing, or crowded branches in late winter.
Offers four-season interest: spring flowers, summer fruit, fall color, and attractive winter bark and form.

description: An evergreen shrub native to western North America producing small, intensely flavored dark berries prized for their sweet-tart, complex taste. Wild-harvested for centuries, huckleberries are notoriously difficult to cultivate commercially.
harvest_instructions: Pick berries individually by hand when they are fully dark and sweet. They ripen unevenly, requiring multiple passes over several weeks.
storage_instructions: Fresh berries last 3-5 days refrigerated. Freeze for long-term storage. Excellent in pies, jams, syrups, and sauces.
growing_tips: Requires acidic soil (pH 4.5-5.5) similar to blueberries; amend with sulfur and peat if needed.
Naturally an understory plant; prefers partial shade, especially in hot afternoon sun.
Very slow to establish; expect 3-5 years before significant fruiting begins.
Mulch heavily with acidic materials such as pine needles, wood chips, or composted bark.

description: A low-growing, evergreen subshrub native to boreal forests, producing tart, bright red berries used in Scandinavian cuisine for preserves, sauces, and juices. An excellent edible ground cover for acidic soils and shady locations.
harvest_instructions: Pick berries when fully red and slightly soft, typically in late summer and again in fall. Berries are tart when raw and are best cooked with sugar.
storage_instructions: Fresh berries last 2-3 weeks refrigerated due to their natural preservative benzoic acid. Freeze or make into preserves for long-term storage.
growing_tips: Requires acidic soil (pH 4.0-5.5); will not thrive in neutral or alkaline conditions.
Spreads slowly by underground runners to form a dense, weed-suppressing mat.
Extremely cold-hardy; one of the few fruit crops that thrive in zones 2-3.
Mulch with pine needles or acidic wood chips to maintain soil acidity and moisture.

description: A low, trailing, evergreen vine native to North American bogs, producing tart, bright red berries harvested in fall. While commercial cranberry farming requires flooding, home growers can produce modest harvests in consistently moist, acidic beds.
harvest_instructions: Pick berries by hand when fully red and firm in early to mid fall. A berry bounced on a hard surface should bounce; this indicates ripeness. Home gardeners dry-harvest rather than flooding.
storage_instructions: Fresh cranberries last 4-8 weeks refrigerated. Freeze for long-term storage. Excellent for sauces, juices, and dried fruit.
growing_tips: Requires consistently moist, highly acidic soil (pH 4.0-5.5); a raised bog bed is ideal for home gardens.
