Commercial flooding is for harvest and winter protection, not for growing; do not keep plants submerged during the growing season.
Vines spread by runners and form a dense mat; established beds may produce for decades.
Top-dress beds with a thin layer of sand every 2-3 years to promote new root and stem growth.

description: A native North American tree producing sweet, honey-flavored orange fruits that ripen after frost. The American persimmon is extremely cold-hardy and produces smaller, more intensely flavored fruit than its Asian cousin.
harvest_instructions: Wait until fruit is extremely soft and translucent (often after a frost). Unripe American persimmons are astringent and inedible. Ripe fruit will often fall from the tree on its own.
storage_instructions: Ripe persimmons are very perishable; use within 2-3 days. Freeze the pulp for long-term storage. Excellent for baking, puddings, and preserves.
growing_tips: Most cultivars require a male pollinator tree nearby for fruit set; some cultivars are self-fertile.
The deep taproot makes transplanting difficult; plant young, bare-root trees and handle roots carefully.
Fruit must be completely ripe and soft to eat; unripe fruit is intensely astringent.
Extremely low-maintenance once established; virtually free of serious pest and disease problems.

description: North America's largest native fruit, producing large, custard-textured fruits with a tropical flavor reminiscent of banana, mango, and vanilla. The only temperate member of a tropical fruit family, thriving in forest understories across eastern North America.
harvest_instructions: Harvest fruit when it yields slightly to gentle pressure and develops a fragrant aroma. Ripe pawpaws often fall from the tree naturally. Handle very gently as they bruise easily.
storage_instructions: Extremely perishable; eat within 2-3 days of harvest or freeze the pulp immediately. Pulp freezes well for later use in smoothies, baking, and ice cream.
growing_tips: Requires cross-pollination from a genetically different seedling or cultivar for fruit production; plant at least two.
Young trees need shade for the first 1-2 years; gradually increase sun exposure as they mature.
Flowers are pollinated by flies and beetles, not bees; hand pollination dramatically improves fruit set.
Spreads by root suckers to form patches; remove suckers if a single-trunk tree is desired.

description: An ancient, small deciduous tree producing large, golden-yellow, aromatic pome fruits that are hard and astringent when raw but transform into a beautiful rose-colored, honey-sweet delicacy when cooked. A staple of traditional European preserves.
harvest_instructions: Harvest fruit in late fall when fully yellow and fragrant, before hard frost. The fruit should part easily from the branch when lifted and twisted.
storage_instructions: Quince stores well for 2-3 months in a cool (32-40F), humid location. Do not store near other fruits as the strong fragrance transfers easily.
growing_tips: Most quinces are self-fertile but produce heavier crops with a second cultivar nearby for cross-pollination.
Fruit must be cooked before eating; raw quince is hard, dry, and astringent.
The high pectin content makes quince ideal for jelly, paste (membrillo), and adding body to other fruit preserves.
Susceptible to fire blight; prune out any blackened, wilted shoots immediately and sterilize tools between cuts.

description: A quirky, small deciduous tree producing unusual open-ended fruits that must undergo 'bletting' (controlled over-ripening) before eating. Once bletted, the flesh becomes soft, brown, and tastes like spiced apple butter. A medieval delicacy experiencing a revival.
harvest_instructions: Pick fruit after the first frost when still hard and greenish-brown. Then 'blet' indoors by placing stem-end down on newspaper in a cool room for 2-4 weeks until flesh is soft and brown.
storage_instructions: Unbletted fruit stores for several weeks in a cool location. Bletted fruit is very perishable and should be eaten or processed immediately into jelly, cheese, or paste.
growing_tips: Self-fertile; a single tree will produce fruit, though cross-pollination can improve yields.
Extremely low-maintenance; virtually free of pest and disease problems.
The bletting process is not rotting; it is an enzymatic softening that converts starches to sugars.
Makes an attractive ornamental with showy white spring flowers and beautiful fall foliage.

description: A vigorous, arching, deciduous shrub producing small, bright red-orange berries packed with antioxidants and traditionally used in Chinese medicine. The mildly sweet berries can be eaten fresh, dried, or added to teas and soups.
harvest_instructions: Pick berries when fully colored bright red-orange and slightly soft. Berries ripen over an extended period from late summer through fall. Shake branches over a tarp for efficient harvesting.
storage_instructions: Fresh berries last 2-3 days refrigerated. Dry in a dehydrator at 135F or in the sun for long-term storage. Dried goji berries store for over a year in airtight containers.
growing_tips: Extremely drought-tolerant and adaptable; thrives in poor, alkaline soils where many other plants fail.
Can become invasive via root suckers; install a root barrier or plant in a contained area.
Train onto a trellis or stake the main stem for easier harvesting and better air circulation.
As a member of Solanaceae, it is susceptible to the same diseases as tomatoes and peppers; rotate accordingly.

description: A tough, adaptable native shrub producing clusters of small, dark purple-black berries with one of the highest antioxidant levels of any fruit. Berries are astringent when raw but excellent processed into juices, jams, and syrups.
harvest_instructions: Harvest entire clusters when berries are fully dark and glossy in late summer to early fall. Berries are very astringent raw; most people prefer them processed.
storage_instructions: Fresh berries last 1-2 weeks refrigerated. Freeze for long-term storage. Best processed into juice, syrup, jam, or dried fruit.
growing_tips: One of the toughest and most adaptable fruit shrubs; tolerates wet soil, drought, poor soil, and extreme cold.
Self-fertile but cross-pollination with a second cultivar increases berry size and yield.
Beautiful fall foliage in brilliant red and orange adds ornamental value.
Virtually free of pest and disease problems; deer typically avoid browsing the foliage.

description: A hardy, thorny, nitrogen-fixing shrub bearing dense clusters of bright orange berries with an intense sour-citrus flavor and exceptionally high vitamin C content. Widely cultivated in Europe and Asia for juice, oil, and nutritional supplements.
harvest_instructions: Harvest berries in late summer to early fall when fully orange. Berries burst easily; freeze entire branches, then shake to release frozen berries, or trim berry-laden branch tips for processing.
storage_instructions: Fresh berries are very perishable. Freeze whole for long-term storage. Process into juice, puree, oil, or jam.
growing_tips: Dioecious plant: both a male and female plant are required for fruit production. One male can pollinate 6-8 females.
Fixes nitrogen through root nodules with Frankia bacteria, improving soil fertility.
Spreads aggressively by root suckers; install a root barrier or mow around plantings regularly.
Extremely thorny; wear heavy gloves and long sleeves when harvesting.

description: A vigorous, deciduous vine producing grape-sized, smooth-skinned, sweet kiwi fruit that can be eaten whole without peeling. Far hardier than the fuzzy supermarket kiwi, surviving temperatures well below zero once established.
harvest_instructions: Harvest fruit in late summer to fall when they yield slightly to gentle pressure. Fruit can also be picked firm and ripened on the counter. Test a few for sweetness before harvesting the entire crop.
storage_instructions: Firm fruit stores 4-6 weeks refrigerated. Ripe fruit lasts 1-2 weeks refrigerated. Freeze for long-term storage.
growing_tips: Dioecious: plant at least one male vine for every 6-8 female vines for pollination.
Requires a very strong trellis or pergola; mature vines are extremely heavy and vigorous.
Vulnerable to late spring frosts that damage new growth; avoid frost pockets and south-facing walls that promote early bud break.
Prune vines in late winter while dormant; summer-prune vigorous new growth to maintain manageable size.

description: A thornless hybrid cross between black currant and gooseberry, combining the best traits of both parents. Produces large, dark berries with a unique sweet-tart flavor on vigorous, disease-resistant, easy-care bushes.
harvest_instructions: Pick individual berries when they turn fully black and are slightly soft. Unlike currants, jostaberries do not ripen uniformly in clusters, so multiple harvests over 2-3 weeks are necessary.
storage_instructions: Fresh berries last 3-5 days refrigerated. Freeze for long-term storage. Excellent for jams, juices, pies, and eating fresh.
growing_tips: Self-fertile but yields improve significantly when planted near black currant or gooseberry bushes.
Thornless stems make harvesting much easier compared to gooseberries.
Highly resistant to powdery mildew, white pine blister rust, and most other Ribes diseases.
Prune out branches older than 4 years in late winter to maintain vigorous, productive bushes.
